title: `OCI permission denied` if volume is located within user mount
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I'm trying to put Podman bind volumes of a rootless container within a [gocryptfs](https://nuetzlich.net/gocryptfs/) user mount run by the same user.
Everything works fine if the volume bind is located outside the mount but it fails with `OCI permission denied` if it is within the mount.

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. create a gocryptfs mount
2. try to mount a volume within the mount

But I [created a repository with Vagrant an Ansible to reproduce the issue reliably](https://github.com/alexanderadam/podman_oci_permission_denied#creating-podman-bind-volume-within-user-mount) since this is probably better to debug.

**Describe the results you received:**

The container isn't starting and complains with

```
Error: unable to start container "…": error stat'ing file `/home/podman_user/decrypted/postgres`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
```
which seem to be [a common error message](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues?q=%22OCI+permission+denied%22).

**Describe the results you expected:**

The container runs.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.4.4
API Version:  3.4.4
Go Version:   go1.17.3
Built:        Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

<details>
  <summary>Click to show</summary>


**Package info (output of `apt list podman`):**

```

```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


No, I don't know [how to properly get a more recent version on Ubuntu 22.04](https://askubuntu.com/questions/1414446/whats-the-recommended-way-of-installing-podman-4-in-ubuntu-22-04) since the PPA isn't available for this version any more.
Is there any new, production-ready and reliable variant to do this?

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):** Libvirt

comments.body:
What is the podman command you are using to mount the volume?

Have you tried with --privileged?

Thank you for your fast response!
I created [an Ansible repo](https://github.com/alexanderadam/podman_oci_permission_denied#creating-podman-bind-volume-within-user-mount) for easy reproducibility and the volume line is [`"/home/podman_user/decrypted/postgres:/var/lib/postgresql/data:Z,U,rbind,noexec"`](https://github.com/alexanderadam/podman_oci_permission_denied/blob/main/roles/podman/tasks/postgres.yml#L31).

I didn't try with `--privileged` since I want to achieve this combination within an unprivileged context.
Please try with `--privileged` - knowing whether elevated privileges solve the problem is very relevant to determining where the problem could be.

`--privileged` doesn't help. It doesn't even work when I prepend `sudo`.

I thought that the Ansible repo would help you to test it yourself.
But if you rather want to setup everything manually, you could simply follow this (_you obviously have to prepare the machine with podman and gocryptfs before_):

```bash
mkdir decrypted
mkdir encrypted
gocryptfs -init encrypted
gocryptfs encrypted decrypted
mkdir decrypted/postgres
podman pod create --name harry-podder
podman run --privileged --name postgres --pod harry-podder -e POSTGRES_USER=foo -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=bar -v "$HOME/decrypted/postgres:/var/lib/postgresql/data:Z,U,rbind,noexec" docker.io/postgres:14
```
Does this work if  you run the container outside of a Pod?
Trying this without the gocryptfs, it works fine for me.

> Does this work if you run the container outside of a Pod?

No, it doesn't. Neither with nor without `--privileged` and/or `sudo`.

> Trying this without the gocryptfs, it works fine for me.

Exactly, this is only happening within the GoCryptfs user mount.
That's why I explicitly wrote in the issue

> Everything works fine if the volume bind is located outside the mount but it fails with OCI permission denied if it is within the mount.

So the mentioned user mount is definitely relevant.

I would figure there is some rootless interaction going on here.

Does the blow up if you switch the runtime to runc?

podman --runtime=runc ...

> Does the blow up if you switch the runtime to runc?

It gives a different error
```bash
$ podman run --runtime=runc --privileged --name postgres --pod harry-podder -e POSTGRES_USER=foo -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=bar -v "$HOME/decrypted/postgres:/var/lib/postgresql/data:Z,U,rbind,noexec" docker.io/postgres:14
chown: changing ownership of '/var/lib/postgresql/data': Operation not permitted
```

and with sudo it gives `Error: statfs /home/vagrant/decrypted/postgres: permission denied` again.
Could yo try to remove the :Z and see if this changes anything?  How about :U? :rbind? :noexec?

It's always `Operation not permitted` or `permission denied`.
The mount options make no differences at all. The error message only depends on calling Podman it with `--privileged`, without it and with or without `sudo`.

But it never works.

Is there anything I could do to make this easier accessible to you than proxying your commands into my VM?

Could you give me podman info?

What is the storage driver?

> Could you give me podman info?

It's included in [the first post](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/14488#issue-1260899749).

You can click below `Output of podman info --debug` on `Click to show` to see the whole response.

> What is the storage driver?

It's [`overlay`](https://github.com/alexanderadam/podman_oci_permission_denied/blob/main/roles/podman/tasks/main.yml#L26-L31)
@giuseppe Ideas?

could you try creating the mount inside the user namespace?

Otherwise it is running on the host, where the user has access to only one UID/GID and chown fails.

What you'd have to do is:

```
podman unshare <------------- brings you in the rootless user namespace
mkdir decrypted
mkdir encrypted
gocryptfs -init encrypted
gocryptfs encrypted decrypted
mkdir decrypted/postgres
podman pod create --name harry-podder
podman run --privileged --name postgres --pod harry-podder -e POSTGRES_USER=foo -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=bar -v "$HOME/decrypted/postgres:/var/lib/postgresql/data:Z,U,rbind,noexec" docker.io/postgres:14
```

> could you try creating the mount inside the user namespace?

It fails with `mkdir: cannot stat ‘/var/lib/postgresql/data’: Permission denied`
Could this be overlay does not support mountson top of the fuse file system created with gocryptfs?
another thing to check: the FUSE file system must be mounted with `allow_other` otherwise other users cannot access it.

> another thing to check: the FUSE file system must be mounted with `allow_other` otherwise other users cannot access it.

oh no! This was it! Thank you so much for going through the debugging process with me.
I'm very sorry that I didn't see this and I hope I didn't waste your time.

Thank you for working on the container ecosystem! :raised_hands: 

**Just to clarify:** `allow_other` is needed because namespaces aren't _really_ working with the same user but some kind of virtual twin. Am I getting this right?
hmmm getting similar issue. I'm using podman on macos, not sure what I do `allow_other` or where to execute. It would be great if someone can give more hint. Thanks

I'm also still getting permission issues with `:U` volume mounts or when trying to do [`podman unshare`](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-unshare.1.html).

But I'll open a separate issue for this at one point since it's slightly different.
Thanks @mtalexan for clearing this out.

Btw @rhatdan & @giuseppe would it make sense and be possible to give a hint with the error message?
Something like `OCI permission denied (ensure that 'allow_other' is set if this happens on a FUSE mount)` to improve user experience?

The error isn't specific to FUSE file systems, so that's not always the fix. I don't this because I was experiencing the issue on am EXT4 filesystem that doesn't even have that mount option. It's also the same error if you don't have permissions to the mount, or to the directory you're trying to mount.
